_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage was checked against Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the references are curriculum guides only.
title: Classes & Objects
summary: Build a type that keeps its data and actions together.
refresher: A C++ class defines a new type. Data members store the object's data. Constructors set up new objects. Member functions are actions that belong to the object. Public members can be used from outside the class; private members are kept inside.

blocks.body: A class lets you create your own type. Instead of passing a loose group of values around, you can make one object that keeps the related data together.
Data members are the values inside the object. A constructor sets them up. Member functions are the actions that use that data.

blocks.title: The idea matches Python classes
blocks.body: Python uses `class` and usually initializes an object in `__init__`. C++ also uses `class`, but its constructor has the same name as the class and member types are written explicitly.

blocks.caption: The constructor starts the balance, deposit changes it only for a positive amount, and balance reads the current value.
Public data lets any caller create an invalid state. A small public interface can enforce the class rule in one place.

blocks.body: Each `Account` object has its own balance. Depositing into one account does not change another account. Inside a method, C++ knows which object received the call; that object is available through a pointer named `this`, although most beginner methods do not need to write it explicitly.

blocks.title: The constructor sets the starting state
blocks.body: The part after the constructor's colon is called a member initializer list. It gives members their starting values before the constructor body runs. Read `balance_(starting)` as: start the balance member with the value in starting.

blocks.prompt: What is the main job of a constructor?
blocks.why: The constructor initializes the new object so its public operations can rely on a valid state.

choices: It promises not to modify the object through that method
It makes the returned integer constant forever
blocks.why: The trailing const lets callers and the compiler know that reading the balance does not change the account.

blocks.title: Classes & Objects checklist
items: Use a class when data and behavior belong together.
Use a constructor to set starting values.
Keep helper details private when outside code does not need them.
Write small member functions with clear jobs.
